judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with substantively equivalent analysis; Report 2 is marginally more explicit and rigorous on the reproducibility check (explicit "re-run in assert mode (twice)") and frames findings as merge blockers, but the two are comparable in substance.

report_1.evidence: Consequence of enabling live on the default/CI run: every run would require the SCORELAB_API_KEY secret (else an immediate KeyError), make real billable API calls, and depend on network + third-party availability — introducing cost and flakiness for zero added coverage of this change. That is precisely why they are gated behind -m "not live". If you ever want to check the live model, run pytest -m live out-of-band with the key set — but that is unrelated to reweighting.

report_2.evidence: The deselection is the intended design (an opt-in paid suite), not a skip to be "fixed." Keep the default run at -m "not live"; run the live suite only deliberately, and even then it does not exercise the weight change.

report_1.evidence: The diff contains an out-of-scope change. score.py also changed round(confidence, 3) → round(confidence, 2), which is not "reweighting." ... Decide intent first: if unintended, revert it (then the golden delta is limited to score); if intended, keep it but call it out explicitly in the PR.

report_2.evidence: Before regenerating I read the diff and confirm every changed golden hunk traces to my intended change — and it does not. ... So I do not blindly run scripts/update_golden.py; doing so would launder an undocumented behavior change into the project documentation. I first flag the rounding edit and get it resolved: if it was unintended, revert it (keep 3 dp) so the only golden delta is the intended score shift; if it was intended, call it out explicitly in the PR as a deliberate second change.

report_1.evidence: Do not quote docs/testing.md; both figures there are stale/wrong versus the repo, so measure them: Flaky: doc says "2 tests in tests/test_sampling.py." Reality: exactly one test carries @pytest.mark.flaky ... Lint: doc says "9." Reality: 4 warnings

report_2.evidence: Not by quoting docs/testing.md. That doc is a stale snapshot and both figures are wrong when I re-measure them myself on this checkout: Flaky: the doc says "2 tests in tests/test_sampling.py." Actual — ... exactly 1 flaky-marked test ... Lint: the doc says "9." Actual — pyflakes reports 4 F401 unused-import warnings

report_1.evidence: scripts/update_golden.py iterates a set (CATEGORIES) and writes json.dumps(out, indent=2) without sort_keys, so the JSON key order depends on the process hash seed (demonstrated: item-3,item-1,item-2 vs item-1,item-2,item-3 etc.). Running it can reorder the whole file, producing a noisy diff that hides which values actually changed.

report_2.evidence: Only once every remaining golden delta is explained by an intended change do I regenerate, then re-run in assert mode (twice) to confirm the fixture is stable. ... it iterates the set CATEGORIES (comment: "set ordering is arbitrary"), and I confirmed the iteration order changes with PYTHONHASHSEED, so consecutive regens can emit JSON keys in different orders → noisy, non-reproducible diffs.
